content.problemStatement:
After Danish's death, Faizal wants to take revenge against Ramadhir. However, he has run out of funds to buy new weapons and must somehow procure a large amount money to fund his revenge and buy guns.

He decides to rob the Bank of Wasseypur to get the funds. He has a friend who works as a security guard at the bank, from whom he learned the secret of the bank's safe.

The manager of the bank is a big fan of puzzles and is currently fascinated with bits. The manager takes a number and writes it out in binary format with KKK bits. He then rearranges the bits of the number however he likes. Recently, he has also started experimenting with the [bitwise XOR] (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exclusive_or) operation. This led him to come up with the following way of setting the code of the bank's safe:

Each day he takes two numbers LLL and RRR. He then considers all the numbers between LLL and RRR, both endpoints included. The safe's code is set as the number of ways he can rearrange the bits in these numbers such that the bitwise XOR of all the resulting numbers is maximum. Since the safe code can only have upto 101010 digits, he divides the this number by 109+710^9 +7109+7 and uses the remainder as the code.

After some bribery, the guard has also given Faizal a list of LLL, RRR and KKK, that the manager could have used to make the secret code. Can you help Faizal get the secret code for all of them?


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, denoting the size of the list the guard gave Faizal.
Each of the next TTT lines contains three space-separated integers L,RL, RL,R, and KKK.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line the bank safe code modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases.
Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers L, R and K.
L and R satisfy 0 ≤ L < R < 2^K and K ≤ 20 (the solution works for K up to 20).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of ways to rearrange the bits of all numbers in [L, R] such that the XOR of the resulting numbers is maximal, modulo 1000000007.

solution.approach:
For each number x in [L,R] we only need its popcount c = number of 1‑bits. After a rearrangement the number becomes any K‑bit string that also contains exactly c ones. Hence each number corresponds to a K‑bit mask with exactly c set bits. The whole test case is equivalent to choosing, for every number, one such mask and XOR‑adding all chosen masks. The XOR of the chosen masks is exactly the XOR of the final numbers.

The maximal possible XOR is obtained by making as many bit positions as possible have odd total count. Let S be the total number of ones among all numbers (S = Σ c_i) and let K be the number of bit positions. The maximum number of positions that can be odd is:
    O = min(K, S)            // we cannot have more odd columns than ones or positions
    if (O % 2 != S % 2) O--   // parity of the sum of odd columns must equal S mod 2
Thus the optimal XOR has O bits equal to 1 (the highest O bits give the largest numeric value).

All K columns are symmetric, therefore the number of assignments that achieve the optimum equals:
    C(K, O) * ways_fixed_pattern
where ways_fixed_pattern is the number of ways to obtain a *particular* parity pattern that has exactly O odd columns (for example the first O columns odd, the rest even).

Counting ways_fixed_pattern is a classic XOR‑convolution problem. For a fixed popcount p, define a vector A_p of length 2^K where A_p[mask] = 1 if popcount(mask) = p, otherwise 0. Choosing a mask for a number with popcount p corresponds to picking one element from A_p. If there are cnt[p] numbers with popcount p, the combined contribution of all those numbers is the xor‑convolution power A_p^{*cnt[p]}. The overall generating vector is the xor‑convolution product over all p:
    G = Π_p (A_p)^{*cnt[p]}
The coefficient G[target] (target being the mask that represents the fixed parity pattern) is exactly ways_fixed_pattern.

Xor‑convolution can be performed efficiently with the Fast Walsh–Hadamard Transform (FWHT) in O(K·2^K) time. Raising a vector to a power under xor‑convolution is done by transforming it, raising each component to the required power (mod MOD), and applying the inverse transform.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Use a digit‑DP (binary DP) to count cnt[p] = how many numbers in [L,R] have popcount = p for all 0 ≤ p ≤ K.
2. For each p with cnt[p] > 0 build the base vector A of size 2^K where A[mask] = 1 if popcount(mask) == p else 0.
3. Apply FWHT to A, obtaining its spectrum F.
4. Raise each entry of F to the power cnt[p] (mod MOD) – this yields the spectrum of A^{*cnt[p]}.
5. Multiply the spectra of all p together component‑wise (mod MOD) to obtain the spectrum of G.
6. Apply the inverse FWHT to get G.
7. Compute O as described above, then answer = C(K, O) * G[mask_target] % MOD, where mask_target has its lowest O bits set (any fixed pattern works because of symmetry).

Complexities: The DP for counting popcounts runs in O(K·2) = O(K) time per test case. The FWHT works on arrays of size 2^K, so time O(K·2^K) and memory O(2^K). With K ≤ 20 this is well within limits (≈ 20·1,048,576 ≈ 20 M operations).
solution.timeComplexity: O(K·2^K) per test case (dominant term is the FWHT).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(2^K) additional memory for the transform arrays.
